meta.title: AI Engineer Roadmap — Adjusted
meta.subtitle: 22 Weeks · Built for MERN + Postgres + Python Background · Real Systems · Real-World Impact
meta.adjustmentReason: Compressed W1-2 MERN (already known), expanded AI core weeks, added buffer per project, Phase 5 reduced to 4 focused domain projects
meta.gpuBudget: ₹3,000–5,000 total — use Colab T4 + Kaggle first, RunPod only for DPO/vLLM weeks

tracks.label: MERN + TypeScript
tracks.desc: You already know MERN. Focus shifts to TypeScript, Docker orchestration, WebSockets, BullMQ, CI/CD, and production-grade patterns you haven't used yet.

tracks.label: ML Fundamentals
tracks.desc: PyTorch, transformers, embeddings, backprop, LoRA fine-tuning, DPO, quantization. Understand what is inside the models you deploy.

tracks.label: LLM Engineering
tracks.desc: RAG pipelines, vector DBs, prompt engineering, evals, agents with LangGraph, multi-agent systems, vLLM inference ops.

weeks.theme: Gap Fill — TypeScript + Docker + NumPy + Neural Net Basics
weeks.note: You know MERN. Skip all basic JS/Express/React. This week closes the gaps that actually matter for AI engineering.

mern: TypeScript: types, interfaces, generics, enums — not a skim, go deep
Convert your payment wallet backend to TypeScript (tsconfig, ts-node, @types/*)
Strict mode: fix every type error — this discipline matters in production AI codebases
ai: NumPy deep dive: broadcasting, einsum, vectorised ops — no loops
Build: matrix multiply from scratch in NumPy — understand the shapes
Pandas: groupby, pivot_table, merge — practice on your own transaction data

bonusRead.title: Why TypeScript Strict Mode Catches Real Bugs in AI Pipelines
bonusRead.why: AI pipelines pass complex nested objects between LLM calls. Untyped code creates silent bugs that are almost impossible to debug. Strict mode is non-negotiable in production.

mern: Docker deep dive: multi-stage builds, layer caching, .dockerignore best practices
docker-compose: orchestrate backend + PostgreSQL + Redis together
Understand: why layer order matters for build speed — this bites everyone
ai: Matplotlib + Seaborn: line chart, scatter, histogram, heatmap
Scikit-learn: Linear Regression, Logistic Regression, train_test_split, accuracy_score
Plot: learning curve (train vs val loss) — understand overfitting visually

bonusRead.title: Docker Layer Caching — Why Your AI Model Keeps Re-downloading
bonusRead.why: Every AI project eventually Dockerizes a heavy model. Wrong layer order = 10-minute rebuilds on every code change. Understanding caching is a production skill, not a nice-to-have.

mern: GitHub Actions: CI pipeline — lint + type-check + test on every push
Auto-deploy to Render on merge to main — set up for your payment wallet
Jest + Supertest: write tests for 3 critical auth routes
ai: What is a neural network? Neurons, layers, activation functions — build intuition
Watch: 3Blue1Brown Neural Networks series — all 4 videos (2 hrs total)
Implement: 2-layer MLP in raw NumPy — forward pass + manual backprop

bonusRead.title: The Unreasonable Effectiveness of Recurrent Neural Networks — Andrej Karpathy
bonusRead.why: Classic post that builds deep intuition for how neural nets learn patterns. Karpathy's writing style is the gold standard for AI engineering communication — worth reading for that alone.

mern: Redis: strings, hashes, sorted sets, TTL — go beyond basic key-value
Implement: rate limiter using Redis token bucket — you will use this in the gateway project
BullMQ: job queues, priorities, retries, dead letter queue
ai: PyTorch intro: tensors, autograd, .backward(), .grad — understand the computation graph
Build and train a small MLP on MNIST digit classification
Plot training curve — does your model learn? Debug if not

bonusRead.title: Redis as a Rate Limiter — How Stripe Does It
bonusRead.why: You will build a rate limiter in your LLM Gateway project. Reading how Stripe actually implements this at scale gives you the vocabulary to talk about it in interviews credibly.

mern: WebSockets with Socket.io: rooms, namespaces, emit patterns
Add real-time transaction notifications to your payment wallet
PM2: cluster mode, log rotation, memory monitoring
ai: CNNs: filters, pooling, feature maps — intuition only, no math yet
HuggingFace: from transformers import pipeline — run sentiment analysis
Build a small CNN in PyTorch on CIFAR-10 on free Colab T4

bonusRead.title: A Recipe for Training Neural Networks — Andrej Karpathy
bonusRead.why: The single best guide to debugging neural nets that do not train. You will hit this problem. Read this before you spend 4 hours on a bug that is actually a learning rate issue.

mern: Add to your payment wallet: real-time balance updates via WebSocket
TypeScript strict everywhere — no any types allowed
Docker Compose: wallet backend + PostgreSQL + Redis in one command
GitHub Actions CI: lint + typecheck + test on every push
Deploy to Render — test live WebSocket connection
Write README with architecture diagram
ai: Watch: Andrej Karpathy 'Let's Build a Neural Net from Scratch' — code along fully
Understand loss.backward() and optimizer.step() — the training loop in detail
HuggingFace Hub: browse models, datasets — get comfortable with the interface
Run: 3 different pipelines (sentiment, NER, zero-shot classification)
Journal: what surprised you? What is still unclear? Write it down.

bonusReads.title: How PyTorch's Autograd Works Under the Hood
bonusReads.why: Autograd is the foundation of all deep learning training. Understanding the computation graph means you can debug training failures that others cannot.

bonusReads.title: The Illustrated Neural Network — Jay Alammar
bonusReads.why: Best visual explainer for neural nets on the internet. Read this alongside the Karpathy video for two complementary perspectives.

bonusReads.title: What WebSockets Are Actually Good For — Ably Engineering
bonusReads.why: Your AI projects will need real-time streaming (agent logs, LLM token streams). Knowing when to use WebSockets vs SSE vs polling is a system design question you will get asked.

mern: Prometheus: instrument your wallet backend — request count, latency histograms
Grafana: connect to Prometheus, build a 4-panel dashboard
System Design: review your payment wallet architecture — what would break at 100k users?
ai: Transformers intuition: attention mechanism, tokens, embeddings, positional encoding
Watch: Yannic Kilcher 'Attention is All You Need' — full video
sentence-transformers: encode sentences, compute cosine similarity
Build: find the most similar FAQ answer to a user question — 30 lines of Python

bonusReads.title: The Illustrated Transformer — Jay Alammar
bonusReads.why: The single most linked explainer for transformers. Every AI engineer has read this. If you have not, you will feel left out of conversations at interviews.

bonusReads.title: Attention Is All You Need — Original Paper
bonusReads.why: Read only the abstract, introduction, and Figure 1. Understanding the original architecture from the source gives you credibility that tutorial readers lack.

bonusReads.title: Prometheus Best Practices — SoundCloud Engineering
bonusReads.why: You will instrument multiple AI services with Prometheus. Understanding counters vs gauges vs histograms means your metrics are actually useful, not just noise.

weeks.theme: Embeddings + RAG Foundations + Vector DBs + LangChain
weeks.note: This is where AI engineering actually starts. Take this seriously — everything in Phases 2-5 builds on these concepts.

mern: Zod: schema validation on all API inputs — add to your payment wallet
Error handling patterns: Result type, never throw in async routes
OpenAPI spec: document your wallet API with swagger-jsdoc
ai: What are embeddings? Word2Vec intuition, sentence embeddings, semantic space
sentence-transformers: model.encode() → vector, cosine similarity
ChromaDB: install, create collection, add documents, query by similarity

bonusRead.title: What Are Word Embeddings? — Sebastian Ruder
bonusRead.why: Embeddings are the foundation of all modern AI retrieval. Sebastian Ruder is one of the best writers in NLP — this post gives you the intuition that most engineers cargo-cult without understanding.

mern: Prisma ORM: compare with your existing Mongoose/Postgres patterns
Database migrations: up/down, versioning — critical for AI projects storing eval results
Connection pooling: why it matters at scale — pgBouncer patterns
ai: OpenAI API: chat completions + embeddings endpoint — your first real API call
Pinecone: create index, upsert vectors, query with metadata filters
Build: semantic search over 100 sentences — no keywords, pure embedding similarity

bonusRead.title: Vector Databases Explained — Pinecone Learning Center
bonusRead.why: Vector DBs are the storage layer of every RAG system. Understanding how ANN (Approximate Nearest Neighbour) search works — HNSW, IVF — means you can tune retrieval performance, not just plug it in.

mern: Streaming APIs: SSE (Server-Sent Events) — implement in Express
Client: consume SSE in React — show token-by-token LLM output
Compare: SSE vs WebSocket — when each is the right choice
ai: RAG foundations: embed doc chunks → store → embed query → retrieve → answer
Chunking strategies: fixed-size, sentence, semantic — understand the tradeoffs
Chunk overlap: why it matters, how to tune it — experiment with 3 overlap sizes

bonusRead.title: Chunking Strategies for LLM Applications — Pinecone
bonusRead.why: Chunking is the most underestimated decision in RAG. Wrong chunk size causes retrieval failures that look like LLM failures. Most candidates cannot explain why they chose their chunk size — you will be able to.

mern: LangChain LCEL: chains, prompt templates, output parsers — the new way to build
Build a RAG chain over a PDF with source citations
LangSmith: sign up free, trace your LangChain runs, see every prompt and response
ai: Conversation memory: how to pass history without hitting context limit
Build: multi-turn Q&A chatbot with memory over your payment wallet documentation
Ragas intro: faithfulness, answer_relevancy — run on 5 Q&A pairs

bonusRead.title: Building RAG-based LLM Applications for Production — Anyscale
bonusRead.why: Most RAG tutorials stop at the happy path. This guide covers production concerns: chunking, retrieval quality, latency, cost, and evaluation. The exact concerns you will face building Project 1.

mern: n8n deep dive: since you know it — build an n8n workflow that triggers on new GitHub commit → summarises changes via LLM → posts to Slack
This is an agentic workflow. n8n is a no-code LangGraph.
Identify: what would n8n struggle with that LangGraph handles — write this down
ai: Embedding model comparison: OpenAI ada-002 vs BAAI/bge vs sentence-transformers
Evaluate retrieval: precision@k, recall — does your search find the right chunks?
Build: same corpus, 3 different embedding models — which retrieves best?

bonusRead.title: MTEB Leaderboard — Massive Text Embedding Benchmark
bonusRead.why: When an interviewer asks 'which embedding model did you use and why?' — this is where you go to justify your answer. Knowing the benchmark leaderboard signals serious engineering thinking.

mern: Build: add a RAG layer to your payment wallet
User can ask 'show me all transactions above ₹500 in March' in natural language
NL query → embed → retrieve relevant transaction summaries → GPT answers
This will be rough. Debug the retrieval failures — they are the real lesson
Push to GitHub, deploy, write a short README explaining how the RAG layer works
ai: LangChain advanced: hybrid search (BM25 + dense), reranking with cross-encoder
Parent-child chunking: retrieve small chunks, return large parent context
LangSmith: trace every failed query — find the retrieval vs generation failures
Ragas: run full eval on 10 Q&A pairs from your wallet RAG — log all 4 metrics
Identify: is your problem in retrieval or generation? How do you tell the difference?

bonusReads.title: Why Your RAG Does Not Work — Common Failure Modes
bonusReads.why: Before building the RAG Eval Engine in Project 1, you need to know what you are evaluating against. This post catalogs the real failure modes — retrieval failures, faithfulness failures, context stuffing.

bonusReads.title: Reranking — Why Two-Stage Retrieval Beats One-Stage
bonusReads.why: Reranking is the single highest-impact RAG improvement after basic retrieval works. Cheap embedding retrieval + expensive cross-encoder reranker is the production pattern. Know this cold.

bonusReads.title: LangSmith Cookbook — Tracing and Evaluation
bonusReads.why: LangSmith is how production AI teams debug LLM failures. Familiarity with tracing, evaluation datasets, and run comparisons is expected at any serious AI engineering role.

mern: System Design: Design a Semantic Search Engine — from user query to ranked results
Components: query understanding → embedding → ANN search → reranking → result formatting
Draw in Excalidraw — every component, every data flow, every failure mode
ai: LangChain: multi-document RAG with source routing — route query to right document
Self-querying retrieval: LLM generates metadata filters from natural language query
Build: RAG over 3 different financial document types with automatic routing

bonusReads.title: Advanced RAG — 12 Retrieval Techniques
bonusReads.why: RAG has evolved far beyond naive retrieve-and-answer. This survey covers HyDE, FLARE, step-back prompting, and more. Knowing these techniques separates junior RAG builders from senior ones.

bonusReads.title: Evaluating RAG Systems — Ragas Paper
bonusReads.why: You will use Ragas extensively in Project 1. Reading the original paper — even just the abstract and metrics section — means you understand what the numbers actually measure, not just how to compute them.

bonusReads.title: System Design: Search Engine Architecture — High Scalability
bonusReads.why: AI search and traditional search share infrastructure patterns: inverted indexes, posting lists, ranking. Your PostgreSQL full-text search background makes this directly applicable.

weeks.theme: ML Core — PyTorch Deep Dive + Transformers + Fine-tuning Foundations

mern: NestJS: modules, controllers, providers, dependency injection
Build a simple CRUD API in NestJS — understand why it exists vs raw Express
This pattern appears in large AI engineering codebases at Flipkart, Razorpay, etc.
ai: PyTorch deep dive: Dataset, DataLoader, transforms, custom collate_fn
Training loop best practices: gradient clipping, learning rate scheduling, early stopping
Implement: full training pipeline with validation loop and model checkpointing

bonusRead.title: PyTorch internals — how tensors and autograd actually work
bonusRead.why: When your training crashes at 3am on RunPod with a cryptic CUDA error, you need to understand what is underneath. This post gives you the mental model to debug GPU memory issues.

mern: GraphQL: schema, resolvers, N+1 problem and DataLoader solution
Add a GraphQL endpoint to your payment wallet — expose transaction history
Compare: REST vs GraphQL — when each wins in AI product contexts
ai: HuggingFace Transformers deep dive: tokenizer internals, model.forward(), logits, softmax
BERT vs GPT vs T5: encoder vs decoder vs encoder-decoder — when to use each
Implement: text classification fine-tune on SST-2 dataset — full training loop

bonusRead.title: The Illustrated BERT — Jay Alammar
bonusRead.why: BERT-style models power all the domain-specific NLP you will use in Phase 5 — BioBERT for clinical trials, LEGAL-BERT for contracts, DistilBERT for clause classification. Understanding the base architecture is essential.

mern: Kafka fundamentals: producers, consumers, partitions, consumer groups, offsets
Docker Compose: Kafka + Zookeeper — run locally
Build: transaction event producer — your payment wallet emits events to Kafka
ai: LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation — why efficient, what rank controls, intuition
HuggingFace PEFT: LoraConfig, get_peft_model, print_trainable_parameters
Load TinyLlama on free Colab T4 — compare trainable params: base vs LoRA

bonusRead.title: LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models — Original Paper
bonusRead.why: Read sections 1, 2, and 4. LoRA is the foundation of all efficient fine-tuning. Every interview at a company doing fine-tuning will ask you to explain it. Reading the paper means you explain it from first principles, not from a YouTube thumbnail.

mern: Kafka consumer: consume transaction events → feature engineering → store in Redis
This is the exact pattern used in your fraud detection project (P10)
Add dead letter queue: failed events → separate topic for retry
ai: Quantization: INT8, INT4, GGUF format for llama.cpp — intuition and tradeoffs
Ollama: install, run llama3 / mistral locally — no GPU required
Compare inference: quantized local model vs full OpenAI — quality vs cost

bonusRead.title: A Gentle Introduction to 8-bit Matrix Multiplication — HuggingFace
bonusRead.why: Quantization is how you make large models run on affordable hardware. Understanding the quality-cost tradeoff is a production decision you will make repeatedly. This post explains it without the heavy math.

mern: Implement: softmax, cross-entropy loss, backpropagation in pure NumPy — no PyTorch
Know these cold — asked at Google, Meta, DeepMind interviews
Target: implement from scratch in under 15 minutes
ai: Implement single-head self-attention (Q, K, V matrices) in NumPy
Implement sinusoidal positional encoding
Watch: Andrej Karpathy 'Let's Build GPT from Scratch' — code along the attention section

bonusRead.title: Let's Build GPT from Scratch — Andrej Karpathy
bonusRead.why: The single best resource for understanding transformer internals by building one. If you have only 3 hours to understand how GPT works from the ground up, this is how to spend them.

mern: System Design: Design a Real-Time Fraud Detection System (preview of P10)
Draw: Kafka → consumer → feature store (Redis) → ML model → decision → alert
Identify: where does your payment wallet architecture already solve parts of this?
Add to payment wallet: Kafka event emission on every transaction
Push all changes to GitHub
ai: Sebastian Raschka 'Fine-tuning LLMs' YouTube — full video (3 hrs) — essential
HuggingFace Hub: upload your MNIST/CIFAR model — write a model card
BERTScore, ROUGE: understand how text generation is evaluated
Run inference on your local Ollama model — test 10 prompts, note failure modes
Journal: what surprised you about fine-tuning? What is still unclear?

bonusReads.title: Model Cards for Model Reporting — Google AI
bonusReads.why: Every model you upload to HuggingFace needs a model card. Understanding what goes in one — intended use, limitations, evaluation results — is a professional signal that most hobby fine-tuners skip.

bonusReads.title: Efficient Fine-Tuning — Sebastian Raschka Survey
bonusReads.why: The most comprehensive survey of efficient fine-tuning methods. Read the abstract and Table 1. This gives you the vocabulary to compare LoRA, adapter layers, prefix tuning, and prompt tuning in interviews.

bonusReads.title: What Is BERTScore and Why Does It Matter?
bonusReads.why: ROUGE is too simplistic for LLM evaluation. BERTScore uses semantic similarity. You will use both in Project 4 when comparing SFT vs DPO models. Know the difference before you need it.

mern: System Design: Design Twitter Feed — fanout on write vs read
Draw architecture in Excalidraw, explain every component
Map this to your payment wallet: how would you build a transaction feed for 1M users?
ai: LangGraph intro: stateful agents, nodes, edges, state schema, checkpoints
Build: simple 3-node LangGraph — Planner → Executor → Reviewer
LangSmith: trace your LangGraph run — see every node's input/output

bonusReads.title: LangGraph: Multi-Agent Workflows — Official Guide
bonusReads.why: LangGraph is the backbone of Projects 3, 5, 9, and 11. Understanding the state machine model — nodes, edges, conditional routing — before you need it saves days of confusion later.

bonusReads.title: Designing Data-Intensive Applications — Chapter 11 (Stream Processing)
bonusReads.why: DDIA is required reading for system design interviews. Chapter 11 on stream processing is directly relevant to your Kafka-based fraud detection pipeline. Read this chapter this week.

bonusReads.title: Fanout on Write vs Read — Instagram Engineering
bonusReads.why: The Twitter feed design question is asked at every senior engineering interview. Instagram solved the same problem. Understanding their actual architecture gives you concrete examples to cite.

weeks.theme: Project 1 — RAG Evaluation Engine (Part 1: Backend + Eval Pipeline)
weeks.note: Projects now get 2 weeks each. This is the correct pace. Week 4 = backend + eval pipeline. Week 5 = frontend + deployment + polish.

mern: Plan Project 1: Adaptive RAG Evaluation Engine
Design MongoDB schemas: corpora, experiments, eval_runs, golden_sets
API design: upload corpus → define golden Q&A → run eval → compare configs
ai: Ragas deep dive: faithfulness, answer_relevancy, context_recall, context_precision
Understand each metric — what does a low faithfulness score actually mean?
Build: run Ragas on 20 golden Q&A pairs manually — understand every output field

bonusRead.title: Ragas: Automated Evaluation of Retrieval Augmented Generation
bonusRead.why: You are building an eval engine on top of Ragas. Reading the paper means you understand what you are measuring — not just how to call the library. This is the difference between a tool user and an engineer.

mern: Init backend: FastAPI (Python) for AI layer + Express (TS) for auth/data
Corpus upload pipeline: PDF → chunk → embed → store in Pinecone
Golden set API: CRUD for Q&A test pairs with expected answers
ai: Experiment runner: accept config (chunk_size, overlap, top_k, embed_model, reranker)
Run eval: for each golden Q, retrieve → answer → score with Ragas
Store all results in MongoDB per experiment run

bonusRead.title: How Notion Evaluates AI Features Before Shipping
bonusRead.why: Real companies run internal evals before shipping AI features. Understanding how product teams think about AI quality — not just researchers — helps you frame your RAG eval engine as a product, not just a tool.

mern: Chunking strategy A/B: fixed-size vs sentence vs semantic — configurable per experiment
Embedding model swap: OpenAI ada-002 vs BAAI/bge — same corpus, different vectors
Rate limiting and input validation — production quality from day one
ai: Judge LLM: prompt GPT-4 as an evaluator — score answer quality 1-5 with reasoning
Build: judge pipeline that runs on every eval result and stores verdict + reasoning
Compare: Ragas automated scores vs Judge LLM scores — do they agree? When do they not?

bonusRead.title: LLM-as-a-Judge — MT-Bench and Chatbot Arena Paper
bonusRead.why: Your Judge LLM feature is directly based on this paper. Read sections 1-3. Understanding the bias issues in LLM-as-a-judge — position bias, verbosity bias, self-enhancement bias — means you can account for them.

mern: BullMQ: make eval runs async — queue jobs, show progress via WebSocket
This is the right production pattern — long-running evals should not block HTTP
Add: eval run status endpoint — pending, running, complete, failed
ai: Advanced retrieval: hybrid search (BM25 + dense embeddings)
Reranking: cross-encoder — more accurate than dot product, add as configurable option
Parent-child chunking: retrieve small, return large context — add as experiment option

bonusRead.title: Building Production-Grade Async Systems with BullMQ
bonusRead.why: Every AI product has long-running jobs — training runs, eval batches, ingestion pipelines. BullMQ with WebSocket progress is the correct pattern. Your payment wallet background makes this immediately familiar.

mern: Regression alert logic: if any metric drops >5% vs baseline — flag experiment
Export eval report as PDF (pdfkit) — include all metrics, config diff, judge reasoning
Write 10 integration tests: upload corpus, create golden set, run eval, check scores
ai: Load fine-tuned LoRA adapter on base model — run inference
Compare: base model vs fine-tuned on 10 prompts — document differences
Merge adapter using merge_and_unload() — understand what this does to weights

bonusRead.title: Regression Testing for ML Systems — Google AI
bonusRead.why: Regression alerts in your eval engine are a production ML concept — catching quality drops before they ship. Google's guide on ML testing is the reference for what this looks like at scale.

mern: System Design: SQL vs NoSQL — when each wins, specifically for AI workloads
Your Postgres background is an asset here — map ACID properties to eval result storage
Design: how would you shard your eval runs table at 100M experiments?
Mock interview: explain your RAG eval engine in 3 minutes — record it, watch it back
ai: Ollama: swap eval LLM backend to local llama3 — compare vs GPT-4 judge
Cost analysis: how much does GPT-4 judge cost at 1000 evals/day? What does local save?
Evaluate fine-tune: BERTScore and ROUGE on 20 test prompts vs base model
Watch: Sebastian Raschka 'Understanding LLM Fine-tuning' — fill remaining gaps

bonusReads.title: The Cost of LLM-as-a-Judge at Scale — Brex Engineering
bonusReads.why: Using GPT-4 as a judge at scale gets expensive fast. Brex's analysis of cost optimization — smaller judge models, caching, batching — is directly applicable to your eval engine.

bonusReads.title: Postgres Full-Text Search vs Vector Search — When Each Wins
bonusReads.why: Your Postgres background is directly relevant here. Understanding when to use pgvector vs dedicated vector DBs vs BM25 full-text is a system design question you will get asked.

bonusReads.title: Data Versioning for ML — DVC Documentation
bonusReads.why: Eval golden sets need versioning — if you change your test questions, old experiment results are no longer comparable. DVC solves this. Understanding the problem even if you do not implement DVC signals maturity.

mern: System Design: Design Google Drive — blob storage, chunking, deduplication
Map to AI: how would you store and version embedding indexes the same way?
Apply to 5 companies today — start the application flywheel early
ai: HuggingFace: upload your LoRA adapter, write a proper model card
LangSmith: review all traces from this week — find the slowest steps
Build: cost estimator — given corpus size + query rate, estimate monthly RAG cost

bonusReads.title: How Dropbox Stores Billions of Files — Blob Storage Architecture
bonusReads.why: AI systems generate enormous amounts of artifact data — model checkpoints, embedding indexes, eval results. Understanding blob storage at scale is increasingly an AI infrastructure concern.

bonusReads.title: The Economics of LLM APIs — Token Costs at Scale
bonusReads.why: Every AI product decision is also a cost decision. Understanding input vs output token pricing, context window costs, and embedding costs means you can design systems that are economically viable.

bonusReads.title: MLflow Tracking — How to Version ML Experiments
bonusReads.why: Your eval engine needs experiment tracking. MLflow is the open-source standard. Understanding how runs, parameters, metrics, and artifacts are structured helps you design your own tracking schema.

weeks.theme: Project 1 — RAG Evaluation Engine (Part 2: Frontend + Deploy + Polish)

mern: React eval dashboard: experiment list, config diff view, metric bar charts
Side-by-side comparison: pick 2 experiments, see metric deltas highlighted
Auto-regression alert badge: red if any metric drops >5% vs baseline
ai: Implement HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embeddings): generate fake answer → embed → retrieve
Test: does HyDE improve your Ragas scores on your wallet RAG?
Log results in your eval engine — this is your first real experiment

bonusRead.title: HyDE: Precise Zero-Shot Dense Retrieval Without Relevance Labels
bonusRead.why: HyDE is a clever trick — instead of embedding the question, embed a hypothetical answer. Read the paper abstract and Figure 1. When you implement it and it works, you will remember why.

mern: Framer Motion: animate experiment cards, metric transitions, progress bars
Skeleton loaders while evals run — good UX matters even in internal tools
Mobile responsive — all dashboards should work on phone
ai: Step-back prompting: make LLM abstract the question first, then answer
Add step-back as a retrieval strategy option in your eval engine
Run experiment: standard RAG vs HyDE vs step-back on same golden set

bonusRead.title: Step-Back Prompting: Making LLMs Better at Reasoning
bonusRead.why: Step-back is a retrieval technique that improves performance on complex questions. Adding it as a configurable option in your eval engine demonstrates you are tracking the research, not just implementing tutorials.

mern: Deploy: FastAPI to Render, React to Vercel, MongoDB Atlas
Environment variables: use .env.production properly — no secrets in code
Smoke test: every endpoint, every UI flow — find and fix every broken thing
ai: Run 3 real experiments on a real corpus (use financial docs, research papers, or documentation)
Generate a real eval report PDF — this goes in your portfolio
LangSmith: review all traces — document 2 interesting failure patterns you found

bonusRead.title: 12-Factor App Methodology — Production-Grade Web Apps
bonusRead.why: Your AI projects will be deployed. 12-factor is the standard for production-grade deployment — environment config, stateless processes, logs as streams. Interviewers at serious companies assume you know this.

mern: Write README: problem statement, architecture diagram, API docs, demo GIF
Architecture diagram in Excalidraw — every component labelled
Record 2-min Loom demo video — show a real eval run from upload to report
ai: Reflexion paper: read abstract + section 3 — self-reflection in language agents
This is the foundation of Project 3 (Autonomous Research Agent)
Implement: simple Reflexion loop — agent answers, critiques, revises once

bonusRead.title: Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning
bonusRead.why: Reflexion is the academic foundation of self-critique loops in agents. You will implement this in Project 3. Reading the paper now means your implementation is grounded in research, not just intuition.

mern: LinkedIn post: 'I built an open-source RAG evaluation engine' — with architecture diagram screenshot
GitHub: proper repo structure, .gitignore, contributing guide
Apply to 5 more companies — the project gives you something concrete to reference
ai: Lighthouse: 90+ score on your eval dashboard
Performance audit: where is the slowest API call? Profile and fix it
Prepare: 'walk me through your RAG eval engine' — practice out loud, no notes

bonusRead.title: How to Write a Good Engineering Blog Post — Stripe
bonusRead.why: Your LinkedIn posts and READMEs are public engineering communication. Stripe engineers are known for exceptional technical writing. Reading their blog trains you to communicate complexity clearly — a rare skill.

mern: Project 1 retrospective: what was harder than expected? What did you skip?
Fix the top 3 things you compromised on — polish matters for portfolios
System Design: Design a RAG Evaluation Platform at Scale — full answer
All components: corpus ingestion → chunking → embedding → retrieval → reranking → LLM → judge eval → regression alerts
Mock interview on Pramp.com — 45-min full loop, record it
ai: DPO paper: read abstract + sections 3-4 (loss function derivation)
DPO vs RLHF: same goal, no reward model, no PPO — understand why simpler
KL divergence penalty in DPO loss — what does it prevent? Draw a diagram.
This is the foundation of Project 4. Read it now so the implementation makes sense later.

bonusReads.title: Direct Preference Optimization: Your Language Model Is Secretly a Reward Model
bonusReads.why: DPO is the alignment technique you will implement in Project 4. Read sections 1-4. When interviewers ask 'how does DPO differ from RLHF?' you should be able to derive the answer from the loss function.

bonusReads.title: RLHF: From Zero to ChatGPT — Chip Huyen
bonusReads.why: Chip Huyen's explanation of RLHF is the most readable version available. Reading this before DPO makes DPO's simplification obvious — DPO eliminates the reward model and PPO loop entirely.

bonusReads.title: What Every Developer Should Know About GPU Memory

bonusReads.title: How ChatGPT Was Actually Trained: The RLHF Story
bonusReads.preview: GPT-3 could generate text — but also said terrible things. Here is the three-stage training that turned a raw language model into a product used by 100 million people.

sections.heading: THE GPT-3 PROBLEM
sections.text: GPT-3 was trained to predict the next token on internet text — which means it also learned from toxic content. Ask it a question and it might continue the question rather than answering.

sections.heading: THREE STAGES OF RLHF
steps: SFT: Human labellers wrote ideal responses to thousands of prompts. GPT-3 was fine-tuned on these.
Reward Model: Labellers ranked multiple model responses. A separate model learned to predict these rankings.
PPO: The SFT model is trained using the reward model as feedback. Higher-scoring responses get reinforced.

sections.heading: THE ALIGNMENT TAX
sections.callout: RLHF makes models more helpful but often reduces raw benchmark performance. OpenAI called this the alignment tax.
sections.text: InstructGPT scored lower on some benchmarks than raw GPT-3 but humans vastly preferred it. Benchmark performance and product usefulness are different things.

sections.heading: FOR YOUR INTERVIEWS
sections.text: DPO achieves similar alignment without the RL training loop — just a clever loss function on preference pairs. When asked how you align a model — this is your answer.

bonusReads.title: Constitutional AI: How Anthropic Built Claude Without Human Feedback on Harmful Content
bonusReads.preview: RLHF requires humans to rate harmful outputs. Constitutional AI makes the model critique its own harmful outputs using a written constitution — no labellers needed.

sections.heading: THE PROBLEM
sections.text: To train a safe model via RLHF, you need humans to evaluate harmful content at scale — expensive and harmful to the workers.

sections.heading: THE APPROACH
steps: Write a Constitution: principles the AI should follow.
SL-CAI: The model critiques and revises its own harmful outputs. Fine-tune on the revised outputs.
RLAIF: An AI model scores outputs using the constitution instead of human labellers.

sections.heading: FOR YOUR INTERVIEWS
sections.text: Constitutional AI is why Claude exists. RLAIF is now standard thinking at Anthropic and Google DeepMind. When asked how to align an LLM without RLHF — this is your answer.

bonusReads.title: RAG vs Fine-Tuning: Meta's Analysis of When Each Wins
bonusReads.preview: Meta ran a systematic comparison of RAG vs fine-tuning vs both combined. The results clarified when each approach actually works.

sections.heading: THE RESULTS
steps: Knowledge updates frequently: RAG wins. Fine-tuning freezes knowledge.
Tone and format changes: fine-tuning wins. RAG cannot reliably change output style.
Deep domain reasoning: fine-tuning + RAG wins.
Cost: RAG is ~10x cheaper than fine-tuning at scale.

sections.heading: THE DECISION FRAMEWORK
sections.callout: Use RAG when the knowledge changes. Use fine-tuning when the behaviour changes. Use both when you need deep domain reasoning over live data.
sections.text: This is the single most important AI product decision you will make repeatedly. Every senior AI engineering interview will ask it.

bonusReads.title: How Uber's Two-Tower Neural Net Matches Riders to Drivers in 2 Seconds
bonusReads.preview: Every Uber match is a real-time ML ranking problem. Two-tower neural networks, ANN search, and feature stores — the same architecture that powers RAG retrieval.

sections.heading: THE ARCHITECTURE
steps: Rider request fires — Feature Store returns pre-computed features.
Two-tower net: one tower encodes rider, one encodes each candidate driver as embeddings.
Dot product similarity ranks candidates — top-K go to a reranker.
Reranker applies business constraints: driver earnings fairness, surge cap.

sections.heading: WHY THIS MATTERS FOR AI ENGINEERS
sections.text: Two-tower models are the industry standard for large-scale retrieval. One tower = query, one tower = documents. This is exactly how your RAG vector search works. Same architecture, different domain.

bonusReads.title: How Notion AI Ships: Streaming, Context Management, and Knowing When Not to Use AI
bonusReads.preview: The most important metric for AI writing is not accuracy — it is time-to-first-token. Notion obsessed over showing the first word in under 300ms.

sections.heading: THE UX INSIGHT
sections.text: If the user sees the first word in 300ms, the AI feels fast. If they wait 2 seconds for the first word, it feels broken — even if total response time is the same.

sections.heading: HOW THEY STREAM
steps: User clicks 'Improve Writing' — frontend shows loading state immediately.
Backend fires streaming API call — first token arrives, sent via SSE.
React renders each token as it arrives — fills in word-by-word.
The full page is NOT sent to GPT — only selected block + surrounding context.

sections.heading: WHEN NOT TO USE AI
sections.callout: The hardest product decision was choosing what NOT to make AI. Spell check, bullet formatting, basic grammar — deterministic rules are cheaper, faster, and more reliable.
sections.text: Knowing when NOT to invoke an LLM is a core AI engineering judgment. A regex is cheaper and more reliable than a 200ms LLM call for structured operations.

bonusReads.title: How Google Scaled PaLM to 540B Parameters Across 6,144 TPUs
bonusReads.preview: Training a 540B parameter model requires splitting it across thousands of chips. The engineering behind PaLM is as impressive as the model itself.

sections.heading: THREE TYPES OF PARALLELISM
steps: Tensor Parallelism: single matrix multiplications split across chips. High communication overhead.
Pipeline Parallelism: model layers split into stages across chip groups. Bubble overhead between microbatches.
Data Parallelism: same model processes multiple microbatches in parallel. Gradient sync cost.

sections.heading: FOR YOUR INTERVIEWS
sections.text: At Google, Meta, or DeepMind: know what each parallelism type splits and the tradeoffs. You do not need to implement them — you need to explain them clearly and compare their costs.

bonusReads.title: How Stripe Uses Machine Learning to Fight Fraud: Radar Under the Hood
bonusReads.preview: Stripe processes hundreds of billions of dollars annually. Radar, their fraud detection system, uses ML on transaction graphs, velocity features, and network signals — exactly what you built in Project 10.

sections.heading: THE CORE SIGNALS
steps: Velocity features: how many transactions from this card in the last hour? This account in the last day?
Network signals: is this email address connected to known fraudulent accounts through shared devices?
Behavioral signals: does this transaction pattern match the cardholder's historical behavior?

sections.heading: THE REAL-TIME CONSTRAINT
sections.callout: Stripe's fraud decision must complete in < 100ms — fast enough to not add perceptible latency to checkout flows.
sections.text: This constraint drives every architecture decision: Redis feature caches, pre-computed graph embeddings, model quantization, and decision cascades from cheap rules to expensive models.

sections.heading: FOR YOUR INTERVIEWS
sections.text: When applying to Razorpay, PayU, or Juspay — referencing Stripe Radar's architecture and comparing it to your Project 10 implementation signals that you understand the production constraints, not just the Kaggle benchmark.

bonusReads.title: How GitHub Copilot Works: From Keystroke to Code Suggestion in 80ms
bonusReads.preview: Copilot processes your entire file, open tabs, and cursor position to generate suggestions in under 80ms. The infrastructure behind this is a lesson in latency-constrained AI engineering.

sections.heading: THE CONTEXT WINDOW PROBLEM
steps: Your current file: included verbatim.
Open tabs: ranked by recency and language similarity — top N included.
Related files: retrieved via embedding similarity to current context.
This is RAG applied to code — your Project 5 implements the same pattern.

sections.heading: THE 80ms CONSTRAINT
sections.callout: A suggestion that arrives 200ms after a keystroke feels like lag. 80ms feels instant. Every engineering decision — model size, quantization, edge caching — is optimised for this single number.
sections.text: This is why Copilot uses a smaller Codex model rather than GPT-4 — smaller model, faster inference, acceptable quality. The product constraint shapes the ML architecture.

sections.heading: FOR YOUR INTERVIEWS
sections.text: Your Project 5 (Code Intelligence Platform) is a less latency-constrained version of Copilot. Being able to explain both the similarities and differences — and why your architecture chose correctness over latency — shows product-level AI engineering thinking.

bonusReads.title: How Netflix Decides What to Show You: A Deep Dive into Recommendation ML
bonusReads.preview: Netflix attributes 80% of content watched to their recommendation system. Understanding how they do it reveals the production patterns behind every embedding-based retrieval system.

sections.heading: TWO-STAGE RETRIEVAL
steps: Stage 1 (Retrieval): fast ANN search over 10,000+ titles using user embedding vs title embeddings — returns top 200 candidates.
Stage 2 (Ranking): expensive neural ranker scores 200 candidates using 100+ features — returns top 20 for display.
This is the same retrieve-then-rerank pattern in your RAG eval engine.

sections.heading: THE A/B TESTING INFRASTRUCTURE
sections.callout: Netflix runs 250+ A/B tests simultaneously. Every algorithm change is validated with online metrics before full rollout — never deployed based on offline metrics alone.
sections.text: Your Project 4 (DPO A/B Testing) implements the same principle. Offline eval scores and live win rates are both needed — offline metrics can mislead.

sections.heading: FOR YOUR INTERVIEWS
sections.text: When discussing your RAG eval engine or DPO A/B testing in interviews — referencing Netflix's two-stage retrieval and their A/B testing philosophy signals that you understand production ML patterns, not just academic implementations.
